2604.20726.title: Exploiting LLM-as-a-Judge Disposition on Free Text Legal QA via Prompt Optimization
2604.20726.paper_summary: This work explores the role of prompt design and judge selection in LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations of free text legal question answering. We examine whether automatic task prompt optimization improves over human-centered design, whether optimization effectiveness varies by judge feedback style, and whether optimized prompts transfer across judges. We systematically address these questions on the LEXam benchmark by optimizing task prompts using the ProTeGi method with feedback from two judges (Qwen3-32B, DeepSeek-V3) across four task models, and then testing cross-judge transfer. Automatic optimization consistently outperforms the baseline, with lenient judge feedback yielding higher and more consistent gains than strict judge feedback. Prompts optimized with lenient feedback transfer better to strict judges than the reverse direction. Analysis reveals that lenient judges provide permissive feedback, yielding prompts with broader applicability, whereas strict judges produce restrictive feedback, leading to judge-specific overfitting. Our findings demonstrate algorithmically optimizing prompts on training data can outperform human-centered prompt design and that judges' dispositions during optimization shape prompt generalizability. Code and optimized prompts are available at https://github.com/TUMLegalTech/icail2026-llm-judge-gaming.
2604.20726.paper_summary_zh: 這項工作探討了在大型語言模型作為評審的自由文本法律問題回答評估中，提示設計和評審選擇的角色。<lb/>我們研究自動任務提示優化是否優於以人為中心的設計，優化效果是否因評審反饋風格而異，以及優化的提示是否能在不同評審之間轉移。<lb/>我們通過使用ProTeGi方法在LEXam基準上系統性地優化任務提示，並獲取來自兩位評審（Qwen3-32B, DeepSeek-V3）的反饋，針對四個任務模型進行測試，然後測試跨評審轉移。<lb/>自動優化始終優於基準，寬鬆的評審反饋產生的增益比嚴格的評審反饋更高且更一致。<lb/>使用寬鬆反饋優化的提示在轉移到嚴格評審時表現更好，而反向轉移則不然。<lb/>分析顯示，寬鬆的評審提供了寬容的反饋，產生了更具廣泛適用性的提示，而嚴格的評審則產生了限制性的反饋，導致針對特定評審的過擬合。<lb/>我們的研究結果表明，在訓練數據上算法優化提示可以超越以人為中心的提示設計，並且評審在優化過程中的傾向會影響提示的普遍性。<lb/>代碼和優化的提示可在 https://github.com/TUMLegalTech/icail2026-llm-judge-gaming 獲得。
2604.20726.author: Mohamed Hesham Elganayni et.al.
2604.20726.authors: Mohamed Hesham Elganayni, Runsheng Chen, Sebastian Nagl, Matthias Grabmair
